coredata.dc:title: Incorporation of carbon quantum dots with PEDOT:PSS for high-performance inverted organic solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen, Dinh Cung Tien
Kim, Bo-Seok
Oh, Geun-Hyeong
Vu, Van-Phu
Kim, Sejung
Lee, Soo-Hyoung
coredata.dc:description: Inverted organic solar cells (iOSCs) have attracted considerable attention because of their ease of fabrication and suitability for roll-to-roll processing. Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS), with multiple favorable properties, is the most actively studied hole-transporting layer (HTL) material. However, the pristine state of PEDOT:PSS is limited by its inefficient, poor, and inhomogeneous conductivity. Herein, we demonstrate strategies to enhance the performance of iOSC devices by introducing carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which have controllable conductivity and band edges, into the PEDOT:PSS HTL. This is considered to be an efficient route for modifying the intrinsic properties and electrical conductivity of PEDOT:PSS. The CQDs were first synthesized through a microwave-assisted process by employing eco-friendly and low-cost material sources. By optimizing the ratio of CQDs incorporated into PEDOT:PSS (PH-G0.05), an iOSC with a remarkable enhancement of 3.90% in power conversion efficiency, was obtained. Using PH-G0.05 HTL helped to improve the contact quality and assisted the hole extraction/transport ability at the photoactive/top Ag anode interface with high electrical conductivity (2.80 S cm–1), good surface morphology (RMS = 7.34 nm), and suitable work function (4.78 eV). Our efforts to develop a solution process for CQDs-incorporated PEDOT:PSS HTLs shows considerable promise for the potential replacement of vacuum-based methods in iOSC applications.

2D AFM height images and 3D topographic images of (a1-a4) PH-Gx and (b1-b4) PH-Rx HTLs coated on photoactive layer, corresponding to the CQDs concentration of 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, and 1 mg mL−1, respectively.

head.ce:title: Incorporation of carbon quantum dots with PEDOT:PSS for high-performance inverted organic solar cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inverted organic solar cells (iOSCs) have attracted considerable attention because of their ease of fabrication and suitability for roll-to-roll processing. Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS), with multiple favorable properties, is the most actively studied hole-transporting layer (HTL) material. However, the pristine state of PEDOT:PSS is limited by its inefficient, poor, and inhomogeneous conductivity. Herein, we demonstrate strategies to enhance the performance of iOSC devices by introducing carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which have controllable conductivity and band edges, into the PEDOT:PSS HTL. This is considered to be an efficient route for modifying the intrinsic properties and electrical conductivity of PEDOT:PSS. The CQDs were first synthesized through a microwave-assisted process by employing eco-friendly and low-cost material sources. By optimizing the ratio of CQDs incorporated into PEDOT:PSS (PH-G0.05), an iOSC with a remarkable enhancement of 3.90% in power conversion efficiency, was obtained. Using PH-G0.05 HTL helped to improve the contact quality and assisted the hole extraction/transport ability at the photoactive/top Ag anode interface with high electrical conductivity (2.80 S cm–1), good surface morphology (RMS = 7.34 nm), and suitable work function (4.78 eV). Our efforts to develop a solution process for CQDs-incorporated PEDOT:PSS HTLs shows considerable promise for the potential replacement of vacuum-based methods in iOSC applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic solar cells (OSCs) have been considered to replace rigid silicon-based solar cells owing to their superior advantages of flexibility, low cost, long-term device stability, light-weight, and ease of large-area fabrication with solution-based techniques [1–3]. However, OSCs with conventional structures are limited by a poor device stability and performance [4]. Inverted organic solar cells (iOSCs) are able to circumvent these problems because the utilization of metal oxides (e.g., ZnO, TiO2) as electron transport layers and air-stable metal anodes with high work functions (e.g., Ag, Au) can lead to high efficiency and long-term device stability [5,6]. Bulk-heterojunction (BHJ) structures of both OSCs and iOSCs of photoactive layers in the device geometry have been developed, wherein hole transport layers (HTLs) with proper modification are critical components of iOSCs for enhancing the power conversion efficiency (PCE) [7,8].

As an excellent HTL material for iOSCs, poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)-poly(styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) has gained widespread recognition; it exhibits high hole mobility (2.0 × 10−4 cm2 V−1 s−1), environmental stability, adjustable band gap, and high mechanical flexibility [9–11]. The versatile processability of PEDOT:PSS in forming thin films does not rely on vacuum-based methods, enabling a roll-to-roll process with large areas of thin films. However, pristine PEDOT:PSS suffers from two major drawbacks: hydrophilicity mismatch with the BHJ and inhomogeneous conductivity [12,13]. In the presence of solvents and/or surfactants, the wettability issue can be easily circumvented by well-coating the hydrophilic PEDOT:PSS HTL onto the hydrophobic surface of the BHJ layer [14,15]. To overcome moderate and inhomogeneous conductivity, the structural features of PEDOT:PSS have been extensively investigated via several impressive processes, including high-concentration strong acid treatments at high temperatures [16], and chemical doping of semiconductor quantum dots [17], Ag nanowires [18], and Au nanoparticles [19]. Although all these approaches were able to form a modified PEDOT:PSS HTL with more homogeneity and improved electrical conductivity, they still have major shortcomings in terms of high toxicity, cost penalties, and complicated fabrication processes [20].

The introduction of carbon materials as dopants to modify PEDOT:PSS has recently been reported to improve its intrinsic conductivity [12,21]. Although the incorporation of carbon materials, such as carbon nanotube, graphene oxide, or reduced graphene oxide, can dramatically enhance the electrical properties of PEDOT:PSS films, these materials are expensive with time-consuming synthesis processes [22–26]. Another promising approach is to incorporate zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into PEDOT:PSS nanocomposites. CQDs outperform the early semiconductor-based quantum dots owing to low toxicity, cost-effectiveness, high chemical inertness, and high (aqueous) solubility [27–29]. CQDs with controllable conductivity and band edges, fine-tuned by their size and functionalization, can be easily prepared through various environmentally friendly carbonization strategies (e.g. microwave [30], and sonication treatments [31]) using low-cost and non-toxic natural biomaterials such as citric acid [32], food wastes [33], and plant leaves [29]. Compared with other carbon-based nanomaterials, CQDs have excellent quantum confinement and edge effects, which are beneficial for optical and electrical properties of various photovoltaic devices [34–36]. The introduction of a minor dose of CQD as an additive into PEDOT:PSS can dramatically increase the hole transporting rates and reduce the charge recombination rates at the interface between the photoactive layer and top anode [37]. CQDs can induce a phase separation between PEDOT and PSS, in addition to creating strong π-π interactions, and enabling improvement in band edges and conductivity of CQDs-incorporated PEDOT:PSS HTL layers [12,38]. Moreover, CQDs are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which induce phase separation between PEDOT and PSS, leading to enhancement of their electrical conductivity [39]. Consequently, improvements in the intrinsic conductivity, work function, and hole extraction/transport capability were achieved, which lead to an enhancement in the performance of iOSC devices [12,40].

Therefore, we present a straightforward fabrication process for PEDOT:PSS HTL, including the as-prepared green- and red-emitting CQDs for iOSCs. Green- and red-emitting CQDs were rapidly synthesized using microwave irradiation without any further purification. The modification of PEDOT:PSS with green-emitting (PH-Gx) and red-emitting (PH-Rx) CQDs was accomplished by low-temperature solution-processing. The PEDOT:PSS HTL with CQDs showed an interface with reduced roughness and improved uniformity, which was characterized by the atomic force microscopy (AFM). The power conversion efficiencies (PCEs) of iOSCs, including PH-Gx and PH-Rx HTL-based iOSCs, were determined from current density-voltage (J-V) curves. The PCEs of the iOSCs were significantly enhanced by 3.90% compared with those of the pristine PEDOT:PSS HTL-based iOSCs (2.51%). The incorporation of CQDs in PEDOT:PSS resulted in improvements in both the electrical conductivity and band gap energy, which makes it an effective material sources for use in energy conversion applications.

Zinc acetate dihydrate (C4H6Zn∙2 H2O, ≥99.0%), 2-methoxylethanol (C3H8O2, 99.8%), 2-propanol (IPA, C3H8O2, 99.5%), ethanolamine (C4H7NO2, ≥99.5%), and ortho-dichlorobenzene (C6H4Cl2, 99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., USA. Dynol 604 and acetone (C3H6O2, ≥ 99.5%) were purchased from Samchun Pure Chemicals Co., Ltd., Korea. The donor polymer, poly (3-hexylthiophene-2.5-diyl) (P3HT) was purchased from Rieke Metals, Inc., USA. The acceptor polymer, phenyl-C60-butyric acid methyl ester (PCBM) was purchased from NanoHoldings Co. Ltd., Korea. PEDOT:PSS was purchased from HS Advanced Materials Co., Ltd. (Heraeus Chungbuk Province, Korea). ITO-coated glass substrates were purchased from Korea Electronics Technology Institute, Korea. Citric acid and urea were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., USA. O-phenylenediamine was purchased from Acros Organics, USA. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 35.0–37.0%) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, beads) were purchased from Samchun Chemical.

Citric acid (0.1 g) and urea (0.2 g) were dissolved in DI water (10 mL) followed by graphitization under microwave irradiation at 180 ℃ for 10 min to obtain green light emissive CQDs (G-CQDs). After cooling down to room temperature, the dark-brown solution was purified by centrifugation at 13500 rpm for 10 min and filtering with a 0.22-μm PTFE membrane. The obtained solution was dialyzed with an MWCO of 3.5 kDa for 48 h to remove the unreacted precursors. Finally, the G-CQD powders were obtained by freeze-drying for 48 h. To synthesize red light emissive CQDs (R-CQDs), o-phenylenediamine (0.218 g) was dissolved in DI water (10 mL) with HCl (200 μL). The graphitization was proceeded under microwave irradiation at 180 ℃ for 10 min. After cooling down to room temperature, the dark-bluish products were neutralized with NaOH solution, followed by centrifugation at 13500 rpm for 10 min and filtering with a 0.22-μm PTFE membrane. The obtained solution was dialyzed with MWCO of 3.5 kDa for 48 h to remove the unreacted precursors and salts. Finally, the R-CQDs powders were obtained by freeze drying for 48 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the PH-Gx and PH-Rx
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of IPA:Dynol (49.8:0.2 vol%) was added into pristine PEDOT:PSS to control its hydrophilicity [14]; the sample is referred to as PH. The modifications of PH with G-CQD and R-CQD were made by slowly dropping various concentrations of as-prepared CQD solutions into PH under magnetic stirring for 2 h at room temperature, and the volume ratio of PH to CQD solutions was set to 3:1; these samples were denoted as PH-Gx and PH-Rx, respectively, where x represents the CQD concentrationss of 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, and 1 mg mL−1.

Zinc acetate dihydrate (0.098 g) and ethanolamine (0.03 g) were dissolved in 2-methoxyethanol (1 mL) to form a sol–gel ZnO (0.45 M) precursor. The precursor solution was then stirred for 10 h at room temperature. The P3HT:PCBM photoactive solution was prepared in a 5:4 mass ratios in 1,2-dichlorobenzene (1.5 mL) under vigorous stirring for 8 h in ambient conditions.

The ITO-coated glass substrates were sequentially ultrasonicated in distilled water, acetone, and IPA for 30 min each and then dried. The prepared ZnO precursor was spin-coated at 3000 rpm for 40 s onto the ITO substrates, followed by annealing at 200 °C for 10 min in air. The filtered photoactive solution was directly dropped onto the ZnO layer and spin-coated at 500 rpm for 40 s in N2-ﬁlled glovebox. After leaving it to dry naturally for 12 h at room temperature, the as-prepared PH-Gx or PH-Rx was spin-coated onto the photoactive layer at 500 rpm for 40 s, following by annealing process at 140 °C for 10 min to form approximately 180 nm HTLs. Finally, a 100-nm-thick Ag anode was thermally evaporated onto the HTL surface under a vacuum pressure of 2 х 10−6 torr with a shadow mask of 0.11 cm2.

Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption and photoluminescence were recorded using a Jasco V-670 UV–VIS–NIR spectrophotometer and a Jasco FP-6500 spectrofluorometer, respectively, installed at the Future Energy Convergence Core Center (FECC) at Jeonbuk National University. Thin-film morphology was confirmed by a tapping-mode AFM using a Multimode-8 (Bruker, USA) with a resonant frequency of 0.996 Hz; the thin-film conductivity was measured using a four-point probe technique (M4P 302 System; MS TECH, Gyeonggi-do, Korea). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was characterized using a Kratos analytical probe (Shimadzu Corp., Kyoto, Japan) and an X-ray system equipped with a monochromatic A1 Kα X-ray source (hѵ = 1486.6 eV) with charge compensation. The energy levels were determined by using an ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) with an AXIS-ULTRA DLD spectrometer system and a source energy of 21.2 eV (He I). The HTL electrical conductivity (σ, S cm–1) was measured by a 4-point probe method and calculated based on σ = 1/(ρ
sheet × t), where ρ
sheet is the sheet resistance (Ω sq−1) acquired from the 4-point probe method, and t is the thickness of the layer (cm).

The J-V characteristics were used to confirm the device performance (under dark and 1-sun illumination) and long-term lifetime stability of the non-encapsulated solar cell devices. These devices were exposed to ambient conditions in air, under 100 mW cm−2 AM 1.5 G condition using a solar simulator (Oriel 91160, 300 W) and a Keithley 2400 SourceMeter® (SMU, Keithley Instruments Inc., Cleveland, OH, USA) in the reserve direction from − 1–1 V with a scan rate of 0.2 V s−1, the scan step was 0.02 V, and the delay time was 10 ms. The system was calibrated using a monocrystalline silicon solar cell as the standard PV reference (2×2 cm, calibrated at the National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL), Golden, CO, USA) before testing the as-manufactured devices. The external quantum efficiency (EQE) of the iOSC devices under monochromatic illumination was measured using a Polaronix K3100 spectrometer (McScience Inc., Suwon, Korea). The statistical PCE results reported in this work were obtained by analyzing 8 individual iOSC devices. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were conducted on a VersaSTAT3 (METEK) system, in the dark conditions by applying Vbias = 0.5 V with the frequency ranging between 10 kHz and 0.1 Hz.

CQDs were prepared using microwave irradiation ( Fig. 1). The G-CQDs were prepared using citric acid as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen source. R-CQDs were synthesized using o-phenylenediamine as both the carbon and nitrogen sources in the presence of a diluted HCl. The diluted HCl induced the formation of sp
2 -carbon bonds, and the levels of doped nitrogen in the core of the CQDs increased, which induced a red-shifted photoluminescent (PL) spectra of the CQDs [41]. The insets of HR-TEM in Fig. 2a and S1a show that the G-CQDs and R-CQDs have a lattice fringe of 0.246 nm, indicating the nanocrystalline nature of the CQDs [42]. As shown in Fig. 2b and S1b, TEM images reveal that the G-CQDs and R-CQDs had similar average particle size of 3.67 nm and 4.14 nm, respectively.

The optical properties of the CQDs were investigated using UV–vis and PL spectroscopy (Fig. 2c and S1c). The absorption peaks of G-CQDs and R-CQDs at 254 and 330 nm were ascribed to the π-π * transitions of CC and CN bonds in the aromatic rings and n–π * transitions of the CO bond, respectively. The absorption peaks from 400 nm to 600 nm were ascribed to the transitions of surface groups, such as amine and carboxyl groups. The PL spectra of the G-CQDs exhibits emission peaks at 545 nm at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm. The PL spectra of the R-CQDs exhibit emission peaks at 600 nm and 645 nm at an excitation wavelength of 540 nm. Furthermore, to investigate bandgap energy (Eg) of CQDs, Tauc plot as a function of (αhν)2 vs. energy was obtained. The G-CQDs and R-CQD showed Eg of 2.65 eV and 2.07 eV, respectively (Fig. 2d and S1d).

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to identify the effect of chemical bonding of CQDs on the manipulation of bandgap. As shown in Fig. 2e and S2a, the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s were deconvoluted into three energy-binding peaks, indicating C-C/CC (284.4 eV), C-N/C-O (286.2 eV), and CO bonds (288.1 eV). Furthermore, the high-resolution XPS spectra of N1s were deconvoluted into three energy-binding peaks corresponding to pyridinic N (399.4 eV), pyrrolic N (400.3 eV), and graphitic nitrogen (401.4 eV) (Fig. 2f and S2b). As summarized in Table S1, the relative elemental compositions of the C-C/CC bonds and graphitic nitrogen of the R-CQDs were higher than those of the G-CQDs. Based on XPS analysis, we found that the selection of precursors and presence of diluted HCl as a catalyst affected the formation of sp
2 -carbon bonds and graphitic nitrogen in the core of the CQDs, resulting in the manipulation of the bandgaps of the CQDs. Furthermore, the G-CQDs have more oxygen functional groups (C-O and CO) than the R-CQDs, which is expected to have a greater effect on the separation of PEDOT and PSS.

The addition of CQDs to the HTL can enhance hole extraction/transporting performance and thin-film conductivity [12,37]. Nguyen et al. reported that OSC devices using an N-doped CQDs-incorporated PEDOT:PSS HTL had a significantly higher PCE than those using pristine PEDOT:PSS because of the improved hole transport efficiency, work function, and electrical conductivity [12]. Liu et al. investigated the impact of CQD concentration on the charge migration rate of PEDOT:PSS films, demonstrating that the introduction of small doses of CQDs into PEDOT:PSS can improve the charge transport rate. Therefore, the film conductivity was increased, resulting in a relatively higher device efficiency [37].

The effect of the introduction of CQDs on the thin film morphology were investigated with CQD concentrations of 0.05–1 mg mL−1 by employing AFM. The PH-Gx and PH-Rx HTLs were coated onto the surface of a photoactive P3HT:PCBM layer. The existence of PH, PH-Gx, and PH-Rx was confirmed by the dark and oval-shaped particles uniformly coated onto the surface of the photoactive layer ( Fig. 3 and S3). The root-mean-square (RMS, Rq) value was used to evaluate the surface roughness of the thin films (Table S2). The PH-G0.05 (7.34 nm) and PH-R0.05 (9.29 nm) exhibited lower Rq roughness values compared to the photoactive P3HT:PCBM layer (13.90 nm), resulting in a flatter and smoother interface with a homogeneous morphology and higher hole carrier transport capability across the ﬁlms, as seen in Fig. 3a1 and 3b1 [37]. However, increasing the CQD concentration resulted in the interface gradually becoming rougher and more uneven (PH-G0.1–1 and PH-R0.1–1) owing to its dense and disordered agglomeration on the photoactive surface, as shown in Fig. 3(a2-a4) and 3(b2-b4). Therefore, an excessive amount of CQDs may have adverse effects on the HTL conductivity, as indicated by the results of the sheet resistance shown in Table S3 and Fig. S4.

It is important to note that the HTL using pristine PH without CQDs may indeed display superior surface roughness compared to PH-G0.05 and PH-R0.05 due to the absence of CQDs. Despite the lower surface roughness, the pristine PH HTL exhibited higher conductivity resistance, which is not desirable for efficient hole carrier transport (Table S3). Therefore, considering both surface roughness and sheet resistance, the PH-G0.05 and PH-R0.05 HTLs, with their low surface roughness and low sheet resistance, are more favorable options for utilization as the HTL in iOSCs. In addition, PH-G0.05, with particle size and surface roughness values smaller than those of PH-R 0.05 s exhibits significant potential for facilitating charge carrier transport routes in an efficient PEDOT:PSS/CQDs network [43,44].

XPS was used to determine the chemical compositions of PH, PH-G0.05, and PH-R0.05. The existence of the S2p and S2s signals is a strong evidence of the presence of the PEDOT:PSS component in the as-prepared materials ( Fig. 4a and b). The S2p spectra of PH shows the sulfur signal of the PSS molecule as a single peak at a binding energy of approximately 168 eV [45]. Additionally, the sulfur signal of the PEDOT molecule was confirmed by the double peaks at binding energies in the range of 164.8–163.6 eV. The S2s spectra presents two major peaks at approximately 232 and 228 eV [46,47]. Notably, both the S2p and S2s spectra of PH-G0.05 and PH-R0.05 displayed a redshift to the lower binding energy region as the CQDs were incorporated into PEDOT:PSS [12,39]. Moreover, an inert information resulted in the reduction in S2p atomic percentages of both PH-G0.05 (7.6%) and PH-R0.05 (7.61%), compared with that of PH (7.71%). In a same vein, the PEDOT:PSS ratio decreased from 1:1.95 in PH to 1:1.88 in both PH-G0.05 and PH-R0.05. This demonstrates the effect of removing the PSS, leading to an enhancement in the connection rate between the PEDOT molecules and CQDs [48]. As the insulating PSS-rich shell region was removed, the π-bonds of PEDOT-rich conductive core region could easily connect to each other as well as to the abundant π-bonds of CQDs, resulting in the reorientation of PEDOT in the PEDOT:PSS matrix and creating highly efficient conductive pathways via strong π-π interactions between PEDOT chains and CQDs [12]. Thus, the conductivity of PH-G0.05 and PH-R0.05 were significantly enhanced, as shown in Table S3. As shown in Fig. 4c-e, PH-G0.05 and PH-R0.05 exhibited full signals with changes in the intensity and binding energy position relative to PH, which implies an agreement with the above results, suggest that the addition of CQDs affects the inner composition of the PH film and that CQDs were successfully incorporated into PH.

After the incorporation of CQDs, the reorientation of the PEDOT chain in the PEDOT:PSS matrix was expected to adjust the work function of the PH-G0.05 and PH-R0.05 HTLs, which was confirmed by using UV photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) measurements (Fig. 4f-g and S5a-b). The secondary electron cutoff edges (Ecutoff) of PH, PH-G0.05, and PH-R0.05 HTLs were measured as 16.52, 16.44, and 16.40 eV, respectively; and these values are lower than the corresponding values of the G-CQD (17.29 eV) and R-CQD (17.24 eV). Compared with others, the lowest Ecutoff for PH-R0.05 implies the highest open-circuit voltage (VOC) that the iOSC can generate [49]. Using He(I) irradiation as a light source, the work functions of HTLs can be calculated following the equation: work functions = 21.22 – (Ecutoff – EF), where EF is the Fermi level [50]; when EF = 0, the calculated work functions of PH-G0.05 and PH-R0.05 are 4.78 and 4.82 eV, respectively, which are both higher than that of PH (4.7 eV) and can lead to a better energy-level-match between Ag anode (4.70 eV) and the HOMO of P3HT active layer (5.2 eV). The work functions of PH-G0.05 and PH-R0.05 are improved by the formation of π-π interactions between PEDOT chains and CQDs, which is consistent with the XPS results discussed above. In addition, a better work function level compared with that of PH could enhance the hole extraction ability and reduce charge recombination at the interface of the photoactive layer and PH-G0.05 or PH-R0.05 HTLs, leading to a significant improvement in the PCE of iOSCs.

The performance of the iOSCs with the device configuration of ITO / ZnO / P3HT:PCBM / HTL / Ag was confirmed by J–V curve measurements under AM 1.5 G light illumination. The photovoltaic characteristics are summarized in Table 1 and S4. For comparison, the reference device without HTL was also tested, which exhibited the lowest PCE of 0.58% with open-circuit voltage (VOC) of 0.21 V, short-circuit current density (JSC) of 6.73 (mA cm−2), and fill factor (FF) of 39.90% (Table 1 and Fig. 5a). The low device performance was primarily due to the direct contact between the high surface roughness of the photoactive layer and Ag top electrode, leading to low charge transport/extraction and high charge recombination rates. Using thermally evaporated MoO3 as the HTL, a PCE of 2.96% was obtained with improvements in VOC (0.58 V), JSC (8.21 mA cm−2), and FF (62.44%). Although a better PCE can be achieved by inserting an ultrathin MoO3 HTL with a smoother surface and excellent uniformity, the use of the thermal evaporation method to fabricate the HTL is limited by complicated and expensive processing and high-vacuum pressure requirements.

To demonstrate the potential to replace vacuum-based HTL, pristine PEDOT:PSS (PH) and CQD-incorporated PEDOT:PSS (PH-Gx and PH-Rx) thin films, fabricated by low-temperature solution-based spin coating onto the photoactive P3HT:PCBM layer, were used as the HTL. For PH HTL-based iOSCs, the VOC, JSC, FF, and PCE were measured to be 0.57 V, 9.39 mA cm−2, 47.05%, and 2.51%, respectively. Since the device was modified by PH, the PCE value increased; however, it was less efficient than the device with the MoO3 HTL. Compared with the above reference device parameters, a noticeable enhancement in iOSCs performance can be achieved by using PH-G0.05 or PH-R0.05 HTLs. The J−V characteristics evidently suggested that the iOSCs with PH-G0.05 HTL exhibited the best PCE (3.90%), which is derived from the improved values of VOC (0.60 V), JSC (12.00 mA cm−2), and FF (53.25%). Besides, the VOC, JSC, FF, and PCE of the device using PH-R0.05 HTL were determined as 0.61 V, 9.49 mA cm−2, 53.78%, and 3.11%, respectively. The effect of the CQD concentration on the efficiency of iOSC devices is shown in Table S4 and Fig. S6a-c. While the VOC remained nearly unchanged when the CQD concentration was further increased, JSC gradually decreased. Specifically, the JSC of PH-Gx reduced from 12.00 to 9.58 mA cm−2 with the increase in CQDs from 0.05 to 1 mg mL−1 (PH-G1), resulting in a declined PCE of 3.17%. In a same vein, the PH-R1 showed a decreased of 2.52% in PCE as the JSC dropped to 7.55 mA cm−2.

These obtained J-V results agree well with the aforementioned characterization study, indicating that the incorporation of a sufficient amount of CQD dispersion into the PH could improve its electrical conductivity, surface morphology, and work function. The higher VOC value can be explained by the reduction in the PSS portion and reorientation of PEDOT in PH-G0.05 and PH-R0.05, leading to the orderly alignment of PEDOT chains and better energy level alignment [50]. In particular, the highest VOC value of PH-R0.05 is attributed to its lowest Ecutoff value obtained from the UPS measurements. However, JSC and FF enhancement is attributed to the formation of a highly efficient conductive pathways via π-π interactions between PEDOT chains and CQDs as well as the improved compatibility in the contact interface between the photoactive layer and top anode. PH-G0.05, which had good electrical conductivity, low surface roughness, and a small particle size, exhibited the best JSC value, with higher hole collection at the anode and lower contact resistance. These results are in accordance with the EQE, leakage current, and EIS (with an equivalent circuit model) in Fig. 5b-d. In detail, compared to others, the iOSC device utilizing PH-G0.05 HTL exhibits the highest EQE (Fig. 5b) and the lowest leakage current (Fig. 5c). The enhanced EQE observed in this device can be primarily attributed to efficient exciton dissociation and hole extraction [51]. Additionally, the lower leakage current observed herein clearly demonstrates the crucial roles of PH-G0.05 in preventing electron flow to the Ag anode and inhibiting hole accumulation at the electrode-polymer interface [51]. The high EQE and low dark current characteristics can be considered as significant factors contributing to the increased JSC in devices based on PH-G0.05 in comparison to PH-R0.05 and PH devices (Table 1) [6]. Fig. 5d illustrates that each Nyquist plot in the complex impedance graph shows a single semi-circle when plotting the imaginary part against the real part [52]. The trend observed in the Nyquist plots aligns with the J-V characteristics, confirming efficient charge extraction at the interface between the photoactive layer and the electrode in the iOSC based on PH-G0.05 HTL [6]. Moreover, this device exhibits lower contact resistance compared to both PH-R0.05 and PH devices, as confirmed by these EIS results.

The decline in the performance of iOSCs with an excessive CQD concentration is attributed to the agglomeration of excess CQDs in PH, forming inhomogeneous HTL thin films with an uneven and roughness surfaces. Thereby, the HTL conductivity was significantly reduced, leading to low hole transport ability and decreased JSC. The observed JSC values from the J-V curve measurements are in accordance with the JSC values obtained from the EQE spectrum in Fig. S6b and d. The VOC and PCE values of the iOSC devices, utilizing the PH-G0.05 and PH-R0.05 HTLs, remained remarkably stable even after 30 days. As can be seen in Fig. 6, the PH-G0.05 device exhibited nearly 100% VOC retention and a PCE value of 99.02%, both higher than the corresponding values of the PH-R0.05 device (99.42% VOC and 98.64% PCE, respectively). This demonstrates the good long-term lifetime stability of these devices. Additionally, both the PH-G0.05 and PH-R0.05 HTLs showed slightly better performance compared to the PH HTL, with a PCE retention of 98.13% (Fig. S8). In addition, iOSC devices using PH incorporated with Blue-CQDs (PH-B0.05) and Yellow-CQDs (PH-Y0.05) as HTLs were fabricated. As shown in Fig. S7 and Table S5, the calculated PCE values were approximately 2.55% and 2.64%, respectively, which were both lower than those of the device using the PH-G0.05 HTL.

Since the stable high-work-function metals, such as Ag, as top anode can be deposited through a solution-based screen-printing technique onto the polymeric HTL [53], our approach using the solution process of PH-Gx and PH-Rx as HTLs in iOSCs is highly suitable for roll-to-roll techniques, where thermal evaporation is not allowed to deposit HTLs on the photoactive layer. Furthermore, the G- and R-CQDs were synthesized from cost-effective and eco-friendly materials using a fast-processing method without any post-treatments; this is preferable compared with using materials that may have exceptionally high performance, but are too expensive and sophisticated to process. The iOSCs using the proposed PH-Gx and PH-Rx HTLs exhibited an enhancement in PCE compared with the MoO3 HTL-based device. Therefore, this approach provides a significant opportunity for the replacement of vacuum-based processes in smart and portable soft photovoltaic devices.

Our developed HTLs combined with highly efficient active layer materials, such as PTB7: PCBM, PM6: Y6, PM6: ITIC-Th, PM6: BTP-eC9, PSe-HD: Y6, could be an efficient strategy for the development of high-performance iOSCs in the near future [54–57]. There are some reports on the iOSCs based on the use of non-fullerene acceptors and new highly efficient active layer with the PEDOT:PSS-based HTL, similar to our work [58–60]. Therefore, we highly believe that our approach of manipulating the CQD-incorporated PEDOT:PSS HTLs can be applied to a broad range of hydrophobic organic photoactive layers, including those based on non-fullerene acceptors and new donor-acceptor molecules, which share similarities with our P3HT:PCBM system. In addition, the processable CQD-incorporated PEDOT:PSS developed in our study has the potential to be used as an HTL to alleviate UV light-induced degradation processes in non-fullerene acceptors-based OSCs with a conventional configuration [61,62]. This could lead to improvements in device performance, UV durability, and stability.

In summary, solution-processed green- and red-emitting CQDs-incorporated PH (PH-Gx and PH-Rx) were demonstrated to be effective materials for the use as HTL to modify the interface between the photoactive BHJ layer and Ag anode in planar iOSCs. The surface morphologies and roughness of the PH-Gx and PH-Rx HTLs were confirmed using AFM images. Surface resistance, XPS, and UPS measurements determined the effect of the CQDs on the modification of the intrinsic properties of PEDOT:PSS, leading to changes in its electrical conductivity and work function. The iOSCs using PH modified with a sufficient amount of CQDs-based HTL exhibited a significant enhancement in both VOC and JSC, leading to a PCE of up to 3.90%, which was better than that of the thermally evaporated MoO3 HTL-based devices. However, excessive CQD concentration resulted in reduced PCE. Given these benefits, the as-developed solution process for CQD-incorporated PH HTLs, along with the selection of materials containing highly efficient photoactive layer materials, is a promising approach to improve the performance of energy-conversion devices.
